topic: color accessibility testing — designing for colorblind users

turns.content: been thinking a lot about color accessibility in design lately. like how many designers actually test their work for color vision deficiency? because i feel like the answer is almost nobody

You're probably right that it's a small minority. Color accessibility tends to be one of those things designers know they should do but skip because deadlines, or because they assume it's "close enough." Which is wild when you consider that roughly 8% of men and 0.5% of women have some form of color vision deficiency — that's a huge chunk of your audience for basically any consumer-facing design.

What's prompting the thinking? Are you working on something where it's become relevant, or is this more of a general design-practice concern?

bit of both honestly. i'm working on a project right now where the brand palette leans heavily on reds and greens and i realized if i didn't actively test this, the entire visual hierarchy could fall apart for like 8% of the people looking at it. that's not an edge case, that's a significant audience

Exactly — 8% is not an edge case by any reasonable definition. And a red-green palette is literally the highest-risk combination for accessibility since the most common forms of color vision deficiency affect exactly that axis. Those two colors can become nearly indistinguishable.

The visual hierarchy point is really important. If you're using color as the primary differentiator between elements — say red for errors and green for success states, or red and green product variants — someone with red-green color blindness sees those as essentially the same muddy brownish tone. The information is just gone.

What tools are you using to test? There are some good simulators that let you preview your designs through different CVD filters.

i've been using the Stark plugin which is pretty solid for quick checks. it has simulation modes for the common types of color vision deficiency — red-green, blue-yellow, full monochromacy. the contrast ratio checker is useful too. but honestly i think the bigger issue is that most designers treat accessibility as a checkbox at the end of the process instead of building it into the design from the start

Stark is excellent — one of the better accessibility tools out there for design workflows. And you've nailed the core problem: accessibility as afterthought versus accessibility as foundation.

When you bolt it on at the end, you end up making compromises — shifting a green slightly, adding an icon as a patch, darkening a red. The design was never built to accommodate those constraints, so the fixes feel like bandaids. But if you build with those constraints from the start, they become creative parameters that actually push you toward more robust visual systems.

It's like designing for mobile-first versus adapting desktop to mobile. The results are fundamentally different when the constraint is baked in from day one.

For the red-green palette you're working with, what's your strategy? Are you considering shifting the hues to create more separation, or layering in additional differentiators like shape, pattern, or value contrast?

right now i'm going with a dual-coding approach — so color is never the ONLY carrier of information. every color distinction also has a shape, pattern, or label backing it up. like if two product variants are differentiated by color, they also have distinct iconography. and i'm paying way more attention to value contrast, making sure things are distinguishable even in grayscale

That's the gold standard approach. Dual-coding means your design degrades gracefully — even if someone can't distinguish the colors at all, the information is still there through the secondary channel. And the grayscale test is such an underused technique. If your design works in grayscale, it works for almost everyone.

The value contrast piece is especially important for packaging where people might be in varied lighting conditions — a grocery store shelf with fluorescent overhead lighting is going to shift how colors appear. Strong value contrast (light vs. dark) survives those conditions much better than hue contrast alone.

Have you found that designing with these constraints actually improves the design overall? I've heard some accessibility-focused designers say that working within tighter constraints forces more creative solutions.

hundred percent. like the dual-coding thing forces me to think about information hierarchy more carefully because i can't just rely on 'oh i'll make this red and that green and people will get it.' i have to actually think about what distinguishes these elements structurally, not just superficially. honestly every designer should design as if color is unreliable because in a lot of real-world contexts it literally is — bad screens, direct sunlight, printing on weird substrates

That last point is really well made — color is unreliable in so many real-world contexts beyond CVD. Screen calibration varies wildly, projectors wash everything out, sunlight on a phone screen kills contrast, and print production on different paper stocks can shift colors significantly. If you treat color as a nice-to-have enhancement layer rather than a load-bearing structural element, your design is just more resilient across all those contexts.

The structural thinking you're describing — what distinguishes elements beyond color — is really about building a stronger visual grammar. Shape language, spatial relationships, typographic hierarchy, iconography — those channels are all more stable than hue. Color becomes the icing rather than the cake.

Are you planning to document any of this as part of a brand guidelines deliverable? Like explicitly including accessible color usage guidelines for however the client's team extends the system?

yeah i've started building accessibility specs into my brand guidelines documents. usage rules for the palette, required contrast ratios, do's and don'ts for color-only information. it adds work upfront but honestly it saves everyone headaches later when some junior designer at the client's company tries to make a red-text-on-green-background holiday promotion or whatever

Ha — the red text on green background holiday nightmare is so real and so preventable. Building those rules into the guidelines means you're not just designing one deliverable accessibly, you're creating a system that stays accessible as other people use it. That's where the real impact is.

Including concrete do's and don'ts is smart — abstract principles like "ensure sufficient contrast" are easy to ignore, but showing a specific example of what not to do makes it visceral and memorable. If you can include simulated views of bad usage showing what it looks like through a red-green color blindness filter, that's even more powerful because most people have never seen their design through that lens.

Sounds like you've got a really thoughtful approach to this. More designers should be thinking about it the way you are — as a design quality issue, not a compliance checkbox.
thanks, it's something i care about a lot. anyway i should get back to actually applying all this to the project instead of talking about it lol. appreciate the sounding board

Go make something that works for everyone. The dual-coding and grayscale testing approach is solid — your users will benefit from it even if they never know why it works so well. Good luck with the project.

actually one more thing — do you know if there's a good way to test for contrast ratios on textured or gradient backgrounds? because the WCAG standards assume flat colors and that's like 10% of real design work

That's a great question and a real gap in the standard tooling. WCAG contrast ratios are calculated between two flat color values, which falls apart on gradients, textures, or photography backgrounds. The practical approach most accessibility consultants recommend is to test against both the lightest and darkest points in the background area where the text sits — if the contrast ratio passes at the worst point, you're covered. For gradients, some designers add a semi-transparent overlay or text shadow to guarantee a minimum contrast floor regardless of where the gradient shifts. It's extra work but it's the most reliable solve.
